course_description:
Design, implementation and presentation of an extensive research project, approved by the Head of School and relevant to the course, with emphasis on defining the research issue, establishing methodology, undertaking appropriate analysis of data and drawing defensible conclusions. The project is completed full-time across 2 semesters.

Students must submit the Research Project Contract to agriculture@enquire.uq.edu.au by the first Thursday of Teaching Week 1 in February for students commencing in Semester 1, and by the first Thursday of Teaching Week 1 in July for students commencing in Semester 2.

Contact agriculture@enquire.uq.edu.au for enrolment queries.

This course is part of the requirements of the program and is designed to introduce the students to research methods. Students will develop skills to undertake the research and report their findings in the form of a research thesis.

Please note these steps to secure a Research Project.

1. The course coordinator will provide a list of potential supervisors and their general areas of research.

2. The student must then contact potential academic supervisors working in areas that they are interested in to arrange a topic for their research project.

3. The student then completes a learning contract with the project supervisor, to be forwarded to the course coordinator.

4. The project must be agreed upon, and the contract completed before the end of Teaching Week 1.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
Completion of 8-units of study in the programs.
restrictions: Masters of Food Science and Technology Research Extensive Students (Program Code 5753)

course_contacts.name: Dr Marlize Bekker
course_contacts.notes: Consultation is by appointment. To make an appointment, please email.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
There are no timetabled activities (except for the seminarﾠfor presentations). Students are expected to be in regular contact with their supervisor.
course_aims: To introduce students to the activities involved in carrying out a research project.

learning_outcomes.description: Define a research problem, state a research hypothesis, and plan a process for the investigation of the problem.

Integrate the practical experience gained in previous years of study to develop a novel process, product, analyse the various aspects of food and nutrition topics; OR undertake fundamental studies, and research data recording.
Design and implement a research project/survey; OR undertaking a substantial critical literature review.
Analyse, process, and interpret research data.
Present experimental results and draw relevant statistical conclusions.
Write a thesis in an approved format.
Deliver an oral presentation of a research project.

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to submit a research proposal and experimental protocol to their primary project adviser to allow assessment and feedback of progress with respect to their level of scientific technical planning, writing skill, knowledge depth and coverage, and comprehension. The research proposal guidelines are below and can also be found attached, and under Course Files on the Blackboard site. Structure of a Research Proposal (~2000 words) 1. Introduction to the topic 2. Hypothesis 3. Aims and Objectives 4. Methodology 5. Timeline (GANTT Chart) 6. References (APA 7th) Introduction (30%) Aims, research questions and hypotheses. Short literature review. Relevant theory is described briefly. Clear and concise statements explaining the rationale for the research are provided. The rationale is both understandable and convincing. The proposal contains clear and concise statements of research aims, objectives and hypotheses. The research question logically follows from the extant literature and addresses a meaningful gap or debate in the field. Methods (40%) The methods and materials are appropriate to answer the research question and the study design and are reliable and valid. The description of the procedure is thorough, logical, coherent, and in sufficient detail to enable replication. All stages involved in conducting the research are clearly described, and the approach is feasible. The analysis strategy (can be statistics or other relevant analysis) is clearly described and optimal in addressing the research question(s). Limitations of the approach are considered and addressed. Gantt chart (20%) Thoughtful and realistic timeline; incorporates all necessary tasks for project completion. Presentation (10%) Spelling and grammar are appropriate, visual aids (figures, graphs, tables, etc.) are suitable for the project under consideration, the format has been followed correctly, and the correct reference format using APA 7th. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Research Project Final Report and Performance
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: See final report format recommendations (manuscript structure) below and on the Blackboard site. An example of a manuscript is also available on the Blackboard site. The final report should be written and formatted as a peer-reviewed manuscript for submission to a scientific journal consisting of approximately 7000 to 8000 words. The report should demonstrate the student’s ability to conduct scientific research and communicate their findings clearly and professionally. Start by selecting a reputable journal in your research field. Review the journal's author guidelines carefully and adhere to its specific formatting, citation style, and submission requirements. Use the structure outlined below as guidance for preparing the manuscript and adhere to the formatting guidelines outlined by the selected journal to ensure the manuscript aligns with the requirements of academic publishing. 1. Manuscript Structure Your manuscript must include the following sections: Title Page Title: A concise and informative title that accurately reflects your study. Author(s): Include your name(s) and institutional affiliation(s). Corresponding Author: Provide an email address for correspondence. Abstract (250–300 words) Provide a brief summary of the research, including the background, objectives, methodology, key findings, and conclusions. Use clear and concise language without references or citations. Keywords (3–6 keywords) Select relevant keywords that reflect the key themes of your research. Introduction Introduce the research problem and its significance. Provide a review of the relevant literature to contextualize your study. State the research objectives and hypothesis (if applicable). Materials and Methods Describe the experimental design, materials used, and procedures followed in sufficient detail for reproducibility. Include any ethical considerations or permissions obtained. Specify analytical techniques, software, and statistical methods used. Results Present your findings in a logical sequence. Use tables, figures, and graphs to communicate your data effectively. Avoid interpreting results in this section. Discussion Interpret your results in the context of existing literature. Discuss the implications of your findings, limitations of your study, and potential future research directions. Ensure your discussion aligns with your research objectives. Conclusion Summarize your main findings and their significance. Avoid introducing new data or concepts. References Use a consistent citation style as directed by the journal guidelines (e.g., APA 7th or the journal’s preferred format). Include all references cited in the text. Supplementary Materials (if applicable) Include any additional data, figures, or appendices that support your study. 2. Additional Guidelines Use a formal, scientific tone throughout your manuscript. Write in the third person and past tense for the methods and results. Formatting: Use Times New Roman, 12 pt as font Use double spacing. Number the pages and include section headings. For those students who have difficulty in deciding what page format to use, the following is a suggestion: Margins: Top 3 cm, Bottom 3 cm, Left 3 cm, Right 3 cm. Paragraphs: Left justified, Right unjustified, Indent first line by 1.5 cm. Figures and tables: Figures and tables should be appropriately labelled and referenced in the text. Include clear, descriptive captions for all figures and tables, positioned either above (tables) or below (figures) the item. Proofreading: Carefully proofread your report for grammar, spelling, and formatting errors before submission. Submit your proof to your supervisor to receive feedback on how to improve your thesis before submission. Student Performance marking The advisor's mark is an objective assessment by the student's advisor(s) as to the overall quality of the student in terms of their scholarship, practical competence and potential for independent post-graduate research. This assessment will be guided by adherence to the scientific practice of maintaining a laboratory/field record by means of regular entries into a Laboratory Notebook. Project activities must be recorded each week in the Lab Notebook, with at least 1 page of the book allocated for each week of activities. A Laboratory Notebook will be available to students at the start of their course. Each student is required to submit their Laboratory Notebook directly to School of Agriculture and Food Sustainability Student Services by the due date, together with the Thesis. The Laboratory Notebook, signed by the project supervisor, must be submitted by the due date, showing the experimental work undertaken during the project. This is a compulsory requirement to pass the course. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Laboratory safety induction All students need to undertake the Health and Safety Inductions and carry out online training before commencing their projects. Laboratory safety training sessions for research project students are scheduled fortnightly and hosted via Zoom. All students will need to: Receive a site induction with their Supervisor or delegate. This must include emergency evacuation exit, assembly area, first aid stations, first aid officer and nearest Defibrillator. Complete a paper based New Worker induction checklist with their supervisor and email to sciohs@uq.edu.au . Complete Training Needs Analysis for all workers (TNA) with their supervisor. Email these to sciohs@uq.edu.au once complete. Complete mandatory online training (identified in the TNA) as soon as possible (i.e. Fire Safety training) through either workday or blackboard. Complete Training Needs Analysis for Lab workers (TNA-lab) with their supervisor. Email these to sciohs@uq.edu.au once complete. Attend a Faculty of Science HSW Safety Training Session To enrol in a Faculty of Science HSW Safety Training Session. To register or email sciohs@uq.edu.au . Learning outcomes: L01
Experimental work Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Oral Presentation Learning outcomes: L05, L07
